result:
The first time I tasted a persimmon I was twenty-three, broke, and living in a city that smelled of diesel and wet cement. I’d bought it from a vendor who spoke only Spanish and gestures, and I remember thinking the fruit looked like a small, setting sun—something that had been captured mid-descent and wrapped in thin, brittle paper. I bit into it standing on the corner of Mission and 22nd, juice running over my wrist like hot gold, and for the first time in months the inside of my head went quiet. Not silent, exactly; more like a room whose windows have been opened after a long winter. The traffic kept sighing, Mission Street kept unspooling its ribbon of languages, but something fundamental had shifted. I stood there licking stickiness from my knuckles and realized I had no idea what country my great-grandmother was born in, or whether she had ever tasted a persimmon, or whether she had ever stood still in the middle of a foreign afternoon and felt time crack open like that.  

Memory is a disobedient dog. It races off after scents you didn’t know you were leaving, returns months later with half a brick in its mouth, tail wagging as if to say look what I found on the far side of the moon. I once read that every cell in the human body replaces itself over the course of seven years, which means I am already three complete bodies removed from the young man on that corner. Yet the taste arrives intact: tannic, bright, almost too sweet, the way an unexpected kindness can feel almost suspicious. I cannot remember the name of the vendor, but I can still feel the exact temperature of the air—cool enough for a jacket, warm enough for the fruit to ferment slightly under its skin. Engineers would call this an inefficient storage system. I call it proof that geography is just another word for appetite.  

We misunderstand desire if we think it belongs only to bodies pressed together in the dark. I have wanted a season, a train whistle, a pronoun. I have wanted the particular sound my grandmother’s spoon made against the side of a porcelain teacup—three quick taps, never two, never four—because those taps were the Morse code that meant summer and unconditional sugar. She died when I was twelve, but the code keeps arriving. Last week, in a café five hundred miles from her grave, a stranger tapped her spoon exactly the same way, and the world performed an instantaneous sleight-of-hand: walls collapsed, decades folded, I was suddenly barefoot on her linoleum, stealing segments of mandarin while she pretended not to notice. The moment lasted half a second, cost nothing, and left me trembling like a leaf that has recognized the shape of the wind.  

People keep trying to sell me better versions of time. Productivity apps, meditation hacks, smartwatches that vibrate when I’ve been still too long. Meanwhile I keep collecting evidence that stillness is the one thing we’re terrible at marketing and the only thing that ever really works. Once, during a thunderstorm in rural Japan, the power failed for six hours. No screens, no wifi, no possibility of forward motion. The woman I was staying with lit one candle, placed it on the low table between us, and we sat listening to water fall out of the sky in sheets. After forty minutes my lungs felt suddenly larger, as if someone had removed an internal corset I didn’t know I was wearing. The candle didn’t flicker so much as consent—yes, you may exhale now, yes, the night will hold you. When the lights finally returned, the refrigerator humming like a satisfied cat, I experienced the bizarre sensation that electricity was the interruption, not the restoration.  

I think often about the difference between a map and a story. A map can tell you the precise distance between two towns, but it cannot tell you how the air changes halfway, how your mother’s voice will arrive inside your mouth when you cross the county line, how the smell of wet hay will open a door you thought had been permanently locked after the surgery. A story, on the other hand, will happily lie in order to tell you the truth. It will claim the road curved like a lover’s spine just so you’ll remember the curve, remember the lover, remember that once you were the kind of person who noticed both. I no longer ask which is more accurate; I ask which one still has room for me to breathe inside it.  

There is a kind of grief that arrives disguised as eccentricity. For years I carried a metro ticket from Barcelona in my wallet, not because I planned to return, but because the ink had faded to the exact blue of my father’s work shirts when I was small. Every time I paid for coffee the ticket peered up at me like a tiny passport, proof I had once gone somewhere and come back changed. Then one day the wallet was stolen. I stood on the subway platform feeling ridiculous for wanting to cry over something worth less than a euro. But grief doesn’t consult exchange rates; it simply moves in and starts rearranging the furniture. I missed that ticket the way some people miss God: quietly, daily, with no expectation of reunion.  

Eventually I learned that replacement is not the same as erasure. I bought another ticket to Barcelona, rode the same line from Passeig de Gràcia to Barceloneta, stepped out into light that tasted of salt and diesel. The city had kept living, as cities do; the sea still slammed its blue body against the breakwater again and again, asking nothing, forgiving nothing. I waited for symmetry to assert itself, for some cinematic moment of closure. Instead I got hungry. I bought grilled sardines from a man who wrapped them in paper torn from a pharmacy bag, ate them standing up, fingertips shining with oil. Somewhere between the second and third fish I understood that the original ticket had been a seed, not a relic. It had already done its underground work: sprouted when I wasn’t looking, flowered into the certainty that I could leave, could return, could leave again. The new ticket went into the trash by the boardwalk, compost for someone else’s nostalgia.  

I keep thinking I will arrive at an ending that feels like a door clicking shut, but the hinge keeps dissolving. The persimmon, the spoon, the candle, the ticket—each one a bead on a necklace that refuses to clasp. Maybe that’s the real secret: not the necklace, but the refusal. The willingness to keep threading fragments onto a string that can’t be completed, to walk around jangling with evidence that once you were hungry, once you were fed, once you stood still long enough for the world to press its mouth against your ear and whisper keep going.
